front: What does LoRaWAN stand for and what is its primary purpose?
back: LoRaWAN stands for Long Range Wide Area Network. It is a Media Access Control (MAC) layer protocol designed for large-scale public networks to enable low-power, long-range communication for Internet of Things (IoT) devices.

front: Describe the typical network architecture of a LoRaWAN system.
back: A LoRaWAN architecture consists of four main components: 1) End Devices (sensors/nodes), 2) Gateways (that receive messages from devices and forward them to the network server), 3) Network Server (manages the network, deduplicates messages, and handles security), and 4) Application Server (processes the received data).

front: What are the three device classes defined in LoRaWAN and their key characteristics?
back: Class A (Bi-directional end-devices): Lowest power, uplink initiated, two short downlink windows after. Class B (Bi-directional end-devices with scheduled receive slots): More power than A, opens extra receive windows at scheduled times. Class C (Bi-directional end-devices with maximal receive slots): Highest power, nearly always listening, except when transmitting.

front: What is Adaptive Data Rate (ADR) in LoRaWAN and why is it important?
back: Adaptive Data Rate is a mechanism where the Network Server optimizes the data rate and transmission power of end devices. It is crucial for maximizing battery life, increasing network capacity, and optimizing the data rate for changing radio link conditions.

front: What are the two layers of security used in a LoRaWAN network?
back: Network Security Layer: Uses a unique Network Session Key (NwkSKey) for ensuring authenticity and integrity of messages on the network. Application Security Layer: Uses a unique Application Session Key (AppSKey) for end-to-end encryption of the payload, ensuring confidentiality.
